sentences.text: Eva Grinston.
E-V-A G-R-I-N-S-T-O-N.
Eva Silberstein, which spells S-I-L-B-E-R-S-T-E-I-N.
Yes.
In I think 1946 or '47, my name was altered by deed poll, and so was my surviving family's name, to Silvan, S-I-L-V-A-N.
No, I don't think so.
The fifth of the ninth, 1929.
Bratislava.
B-R-A-T-I-S-L-A-V-A.
Czechoslovakia.
My recollections start, I think, at approximately age five-- four, five.
I had a very happy childhood, a sunny childhood I think, in more ways than one.
Because I lived in a very lovely environment, in a very attractive house, which belonged to my grandparents.
And it was an added joy to my childhood that my grandparents lived downstairs.
And we were above them.
So I had a-- a very close connection.
And I have to correct this now, because I never knew my grandfather, only my grandmother, who lived, as I say, below our home.
Our house was surrounded by a very nice garden.

And my sister, Vera, who was two years younger than I-- barely two years-- and I spent a lot of our time, in fact, in that garden, which was big enough for us to have private corners and very, very precious places for ourselves.
I had very caring parents, a very devoted, dedicated mother.
My grandmother lived with two maiden aunts, her daughters.
Again, they were spoiling me and behaving in a wonderful fashion to me.
My constant companion, apart from my mother, was a fraulein, who looked after me.
And I think she was there from as long as I can remember, to the very period when we could no longer afford to have her, which might have been at age six.
I can only tell you-- I thank you for that comment.
But I think I didn't have any chance really to develop all that fully at all.
I recall that my schooling began in a Jewish elementary school.
I never went to a kindergarten.
That was not known, or perhaps not chosen by my parents.
The school was a Jewish one, run on private money.
I don't know how.
I don't know-- it had an endowment.
And it was run.
And it was what I presume would be a progressive conservative mixture, rather leaning to the conservative.
It was within walking distance from home.
And I recall absolute joy when I first went to school.
Because I was easy socializing.
And I looked forward tremendously to being with other children, which I became aware of that need when I first started school.
I also, in response to your comment as to my talents, had a talent of getting myself into trouble.
And I know that on my first day at school, I took a pair of scissors, because a little girl next to me complained that her dress was nowhere as pretty as mine.
And I was dead set on helping her decorate herself, and cut great big holes in her dress, and threaded my hair ribbon through, which resulted in a very unpleasant court case for my father.
So my first school day is significant, I think.
I learned a very bitter lesson.
I was at school from age six, which was normal for us to start school, to the age of about 13.
But this was not in the same sense as school is considered or followed on here.
We had elementary school, which I finished.
And then, when it got to the stage of starting secondary schooling, I sat for an entry exam into a girls' high school, which I passed with flying colors.
And I was really on cloud nine.
So was my mother.

And a week later, we were notified-- I think that was my first realization what I was facing-- that on grounds of my Jewishness, I was refused, in spite of the fact that I had passed all their set requirements.

And then there was no other schooling possible for me than that of a continuation of the same public school that I had been to, or elementary school, which set up three more classes to somehow give us a chance to continue our education.

That also came to an end in the so-called eights class, which was probably third year high school, when the Germans decreed that classes of maximum numbers-- I cannot recall how many, because we were pushing the walls out-- were still not big enough.
And they put the orthodox, the Yeshiva children, and us together.
In other words, we became girls separate from boys.
And the classes were enormous.
And the learning really was of no significance anymore.
So actually, I finished school in '44.
I really don't think I was conscious of it.
My father and mother had a number of non-Jewish friends.
And because of my father's professional status-- he was a lawyer-- he mixed and had some very nice non-Jewish friends-- my mother even more so.
And we socialized to the degree that it was known in those days to socialize with those families.
It wasn't a close relationship.
But I didn't know any other.
So I suppose that was the extent I accepted social interaction.
And I really wasn't conscious, because it was kept away from me to a great degree, until the age of about 11.
I wasn't very physically, bodily, aware of anti-Semitism.
I think I became aware.
It hit with a clang, in 1939, that something was very wrong.
Because we were having a family Seder.

And my father, who had been in the army, in World War I, had made a very personal friendship with a most unlikely man, who was certainly nowhere near my father's educational equal, who knocked on our door, and came to warn my father in the middle of our Seder that he was blacklisted.
The man that came to us was a member of the Nazi party.
And he came to warn my father that because of his known [NON-ENGLISH] involvement, he was branded anti-social, Zionistic, whatever.
And he gave my father the chance to disappear.
My father had a passport.
And he left a week after Pesach, in '39, which brought things down, I suppose, into a different dimension altogether.
Because from that day on, it was mother, sister, and I.
And our lives became very different.
In the beginning, I suppose, the shock value and the loss my father's absence created was so strong that I didn't realize the struggles my mother had to go through.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My grandmother was no longer alive.
One of my aunts, my mother's youngest sister, was sent to England.
She was a very bright woman and got somehow into Cambridge.
And her dispatch and sending away took a lot of energy from in the family.
And then my mother, grandmother, and aunt were the ones who were sort of busying themselves around me and trying to fill the gap my father's absence had left.
And really, naive as we were, we thought father would be away for a few weeks and come back.
That, of course, never happened.
And my father went to Palestine and stayed there through the war.
My mother was very, very intelligent and very educated.
But I don't think she was a great survivor in the sense that she was not prepared for this.

And it took a lot of advice from very close relatives, and devoted relatives, to help her through this very difficult initial period, which was made more difficult because we had to leave our original home, simply because there were only three of us, and there was a colossal shortage of accommodation.
And I must say that looking back, the house we lived in had six apartments.
And we were in every one of them.
We were shunted from one to the other until finally we left that and went to the ghetto.
The continuous changes and moving within that building kept my mother frantically, frenetically busy and exhausted.
And she was one of those parents who did her utmost to keep her children shielded at all times.

So really, I still could continue a kind of life, although my father was away, and although the clouds were pretty obviously gathering, and my mother knew that not being economically of any use to the country, she would be in trouble once some heavy persecution started.
And she and others were aware of the annexation and end of Austria.
I wasn't-- or maybe I didn't want to be.
Numbers is something I'm not particularly reliable on.
I think there were 60,000.
But I'm not absolutely sure.
Yes, I am very aware of it, because my maternal grandfather was known to be one of the most brilliant men in Slovakia in law.
He was also the head of the Jewish community for many, many years.
He was held in great esteem.
And there is, to my amazement, a lot of survived documentation on him, in Slovakia, at this point.
I was brought up in what is probably conservative-- leaning towards the very liberal Jewish style.
I never knew my grandfather.
But my mother talked of him a great deal.
And in his spirit, I suppose she tackled my Jewish education, such as it was.
My father was not very religious.
He came from a much more orthodox background than my mother.
But I think he sort of gradually eased off, went along with my mother's symbols and observance, which was not, strangely enough, Shabbat.
We only had all the holidays.
And I can remember going with my father, and my grandmother and mother, to the synagogue.
But Friday was not observed.
And I know that my mother was a very strongly conscious Jewish woman, who instilled that in me to the very end, really.
And I also know that there was a possibility, more than once, to have an out by conversion.
My mother was violently against it, and made it quite, quite known to my sister and I, who maybe would have benefited at times from being converts, that this was not going to be an option.
No, I am afraid I have to correct that.
There was barely two years between us.
And we were very healthy siblings who fought a lot.
I really didn't have a chance to develop a proper relationship with her.
We were a very, very different.
My sister developed a fantasy world, centering around Palestine.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She dreamed of Palestine.
And knowing that my father was there, this got fed on the little bits that we knew about Palestine from probably underground infiltration of various-- what are they called, the people who come?
I've lost the names, suddenly.
[NON-ENGLISH], who came across.
And she built that up into colossal extent.
Maybe it was my probably lesser strong character that went the opposite way.
I, at that stage, felt why am I to make such a thing of being Jewish when I am being hounded for it?

And I just probably, really instinctively, went the other way, so much so that I very much remember that I resented strongly that I couldn't do this, that, and the other, and would have loved to cheat and get into movies under false pretenses, which would have necessitated pretending to be non-Jewish.
My sister would have never done that.
My sister was very sporty.
I was very clumsy.
So we had very real differences.
Probably we would have gone into fire for each other.
But that was probably never tested, or put to the test.
We had a fair few fights.
She was very, very pretty in a very strongly Jewish way.
I was always bemoaning the fact that I had sparse hair.
She has-- she had a wonderful head of hair.
And one of the joys I do recall-- the closeness was when I could brush it.
She hated it, but I loved doing it.
Yes, I do, thanks to my father.
I found some drawings that she had done the year-- probably very close to the end of her life, which reflects an imagined group of Jewish children in a kind of [NON-ENGLISH], a conference.
Yes, yes.
I suppose the first time, when things became very, very different-- first of all, we had to leave our home and go to a ghetto.
And in the ghetto, my mother, who was just such a wonderful homemaker, tried, in that bleak, horrible building, to make a home for us, and did.
We had two rooms that had everything in it-- kitchen, bathroom, the lot-- and an endlessly long corridor, which she sort of encouraged us to use as a sports ground, which was so incredible.
We ran up and down, and pretended we were having physical education, and all sorts of things.

In '42, my mother struggled to keep going and not to be [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] taken to Poland-- came to a very dangerous point, at which they rounded us up, because we were Jews, and we were three females.
My mother's sister, Susan, was a lawyer.
She lived with us.
And she, together with some other relatives, somehow marshaled enough help to try and get us out after a fortnight in a sort of a camp.
I suppose it was a holding situation.
In Bratislava, in a disused factory.
I think the rude shock came there, when my mother, whom I absolutely idealized and idolized, was continuously called to clean toilets.
And I had never seen my mother engaged in an activity of this sort.
And it was very painful for me to see.
I realized then that there was a total hopeless situation coming.
I wasn't aware what, but I could see the way they treated us that things could only get very ugly.
And my mother had a note smuggled in to her by someone which said that the outside part of the family managed to persuade the powers to be, probably with heavy financial help, to get us out.
And we were also told that a dignitary, Slovak dignitary, was coming to inspect the camp.
And he will be calling out the names of those that will be released.
My mother drilled Vera and I how to behave, that we were to hold her hands, and we will be called.
And we just, as quietly as we can, have to leave.
When the moment came-- and outside that building there were railway lines.
It was a, disused until then, railway siding.
And the wagons were ready to take the first lot of Jews away.

The men came in and after a few charming words, which you can imagine, he called the Jewess Elizabeth Silberstein, and the Jewess Eva, and the Jewess Vera, at which point my mother, holding our hands, wanted to yank us forward.
And another woman with two children stepped up too.
There was a momentary confusion.
And it was our luck that the office bearer who came to inspect, who was the minister for the interior, wanted this confusion clarified.

And it transpired that a Jewish person, a go between with the Germans, sold our release papers to another woman with two children for more money, and was quite morally prepared to let us go to the wagons.
And I think the shock horror of that suddenly made me grow up properly.
I realized then that the situation was hopeless.
If one of us became such scum, there was very little to hope for.
We were, however, released.
And I forever pity the poor woman whose family, or who herself, wanted to pretend to be us.
And who knows whether she ever made it.
I don't know.
We then were indeed released and went back to the ghetto for another two years.
Strange years-- because we lived in a totally closed group of people in one house, total strangers, the most incredible melange of people, who became actually quite well-acquainted, quite close.
I'm pretty sure my sex education began there and then, although my mother had told me everything much earlier, because everything happened under our very eyes.
And it was until things became very, very bad again.
There was a lull in deportations.
And in '45, they started again.
No.
The factory was a holding camp.
I was taken there from my family home.
The second ghetto was the old part of Bratislava, which was sectioned off from point A to point X.
We were very close, actually, to the rather attractive, and still existing, old part of Bratislava.
It was called [?
Bastai ?] Street, [?
Bastova, ?] as was I think number three.
It was very close to the actual good part and very lovely part of the city.
And by strange coincidence, my aunt who went with us worked around the corner in the free part, in the bank, as a lawyer still.
We wore stars by then and lived in this building.
Very difficult, because in each flat, each room housed a unit of a family.
So for me to give you a figure at the moment would be difficult.
It was a house built around a large courtyard.
And there were walkways around it.
It was two stories-- a very old, very solidly built house.
I would think about 50, 60 people were there.
Children and adults.
It was difficult because we had restrictions.
For instance, I recall vividly that milk was only available from X to Z.
And that was, I think, in the span of one hour.
And this was my duty.
And I was becoming neurotic and, in fact, became a very nervous child.
Because I used to wake up all night thinking, it's time for me to go and get milk.
My mother needed milk.

What I didn't tell you was that prior to our going to the ghetto-- from all the stresses and strains, having been to this holding camp, and the stress of treatment that she had to live with-- my mother developed meningitis and was in hospital for many, many weeks.
And my sister and I were alone, totally alone.
I don't know how we survived.
My aunt, of course, helped.
But she was at work.
And we had to look after our own needs, and shopping, and food.
So I became very, very locked in on the thought of queuing and going on time.
And I became neurotic-- really neurotic-- and developed a skin condition, which became a curse to my life, psoriasis, which was strongly linked to that nervous tension.
And we didn't go hungry.

When my mother recovered-- and she did, very slowly, with lots of setbacks-- but I do think food, and cooking, and nutrition, and our health was so important to her that her will to be a mother pulled her through.
And I remember we had perhaps one-dish meals.
But they were very good.
And I was a very good eater.
And as long as it filled my stomach, I wasn't very worried.
We really didn't go hungry.
I would lie to you if I said we did.
No.
No, there was total freedom of movement-- only a time restriction.
After 5 o'clock, we were not allowed to be in the street, which often had problems.
Because if you still worked, which none of us did-- my mother didn't-- and your workplace would have been physically difficult to get back from, it was a nervous-- a war of nerves.
Because A, you were a star.
You were very conspicuous.
B, if you took it off, you were in trouble to mingle with the populace and to get home.
Transport was difficult.
So I do even-- I recall having two or three times great, great nervous tension getting home.
Because I had private tuition.
My mother arranged for various subjects to be taught to me at various places, by various people.
And on two occasions I was caught and terrified I might get nabbed.
Because then, of course, there was quite a lot of surveillance after hours.
But otherwise, we were able to move.
Mind you, wearing the stars, and being so very obvious, wasn't a very great catalyst to moving and mingling.
We just moved as much as we absolutely had to.
However, the courtyard where we lived was a blessing.
Because those children that were in the building were marshaled into all sorts of activities by the parents.
And we had a make shift table tennis situation.
And my sister was by far the best and the champion.
So we were not absolutely-- the word ghetto was physical, but not too restrictive.
We couldn't possibly visualize escaping early-- early deportation if we stayed where we were.
My mother was advised by all and sundry that she should change her place of living and disappear.
We tried to find some suitable place for the three-- four of us.
My aunt was always with us.
My mother's sister was a polio victim as a child-- had a very bad limp.
She was my mother's constant companion.
And they were very close.
So four of us had to find a hiding place.
And that seemed totally impossible.
The bank where my aunt worked offered to hide two people in their vaults.
My aunt and my mother were the ones that were suggested to go.
My mother and that family counsel felt it might be easier to split and to hide my sister and me somewhere else.
And a strange thing happened.

The janitor of the building where we actually lived, who was like-- I suppose the definition would be a [GERMAN], which meant he affiliated with the Germans-- was so good natured that he persuaded his rather less good natured wife to consider taking us in for no payment, to hide us.
I mean, time frame was something nobody thought of.
And perhaps if he had known fully the risk factor-- he wasn't a very smart fellow.
He was a janitor all his life.
And maybe he-- he didn't think it through very well.
Because our house was confiscated by the German Wehrmacht as soon as they came into Bratislava.
It was a significantly more beautiful house than average.
And they knew what they wanted.
It was a duel risk.
We were supposed to be in the lion's den.
And possibly that might have been a better hiding place, but also terribly risky.
So until the very last moment, which I think was sometime end of July, August we didn't--
Of 1944-- we didn't really make use of this.
When the separation moment became necessary, my mother and aunt disappeared into the vaults of the bank with some other people that the bank wanted to-- it was very much like Anne Frank, really.

And my sister and I were taken, at night, to the janitor's house, where we were literally put, during the day, under the [NON-ENGLISH], into their bed, which housed all their smells and all their personal life, I guess.
And we were covered with a [NON-ENGLISH], with an airlock, and had to lie still the whole day.
Well, I guess it was a great test for us.
We could only play word games and often got on each other's nerves to a point of despair.
What sustained us was our mother's contact by letter, which she sent to us.
And I do have a letter of hers.
But I am unable to talk about that.
It's too painful.
And we were there.
We were fed well.
At night, we were let out of the bed.
And we could use the outside toilet.
It was getting cold.
We could read books during the night.
So we-- our body clock completely became haywire.
And we turned around and we hated being there.
We really did.
Although they were very, very decent.
And I would like to call them righteous gentiles.
I think it must have been about three weeks.
But time has so little meaning, looking back.
Because part of that time we were also in the laundry of this-- the building where the Germans were hidden, in a crate.
When he became afraid-- his name was Gustav.

And when Gustav became afraid that he might have some visitors, or whatever, he would carry us on his back, covered with a sack, or in a sack, into the cellar, where we were in a big crate of potatoes.
People hoarded vegetables for the winter months.
And we were put into that sort of situation for, on and off, I think 10, 12 days.
And I can-- I really can't tell how long we were in each situation.
We almost preferred the cellar, because there was a piece of light there, and we could read and strain our eyes [? dually ?] during the day.
